elements.sent-2411-001: We give formulas for the conjugated motivic Milnor basis of the mod 2 motivic Steenrod algebra.
elements.sent-2411-002: In recent years, motivic methods have found significant progress and applications.
elements.sent-2411-003: The Steenrod algebra and the Adams spectral sequence have extended to the motivic context \cite{Voev,DI, IWX , which provide new tools for computing motivic homotopy groups.}
elements.sent-2411-004: Despite this progress, a fully explicit product formula for the motivic Milnor basis of the motivic Steenrod algebra has not yet been achieved over a general base field.
elements.sent-2411-005: It turns out that the motivic computation of such a formula is much more complex than the classical case by \cite{Mil , for several reasons.}
elements.sent-2411-006: The motivic cohomology of a point can be complicated, and contains extra gradings.
elements.sent-2411-007: The motivic dual Steenrod algebra is in general a Hopf algebroid; therefore there is an asymmetry in the action of the unit map.
elements.sent-2411-008: The motivic dual Steenrod algebra is not a free polynomial algebra.
elements.sent-2411-009: Previous work by Kylling \cite{Kyl gives some recursive formulas in specific cases.}
elements.sent-2411-010: In this paper, we work out general formulas for the motivic Milnor basis of the mod 2 motivic Steenrod algebra.
elements.sent-2411-011: This complete formula also provides the foundation for machine-assisted computations of the $\mathbb R$-motivic Adams spectral sequence.
elements.sent-2411-012: We work over a field $k$ and work $2$-primarily\footnote{As mentioned in \cite{Kyl , the results also work for an essentially smooth scheme over a field, or more generally a base scheme with no points of residue characteristic two.. .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: We record some results of the motivic dual Steenrod algebra; these results can be found in \cite{Voev, Voe11, Riou, HKO, Voe03 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Let $\mathbb M_2:= H_{** (\Spec k;\mathbb Z/2)$ denote the motivic mod $2$ cohomology of a point.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Here $K^M_*(k)$ denote the Milnor $K$ theory, which by definition has $K^M_1(k)=k^\times/(k^\times)^2.$ In particular, there is an element called $\rho:=[-1]\in K^M_1(k)\subset (\mathbb M_2)_{-1,-1}$; the element $\rho$ is possibly zero if $-1$ is a square in $k$.
elements.sent-2411-017: The motivic dual Steenrod algebra is \begin{equation}\label{eq:dualA} A_{**}=\mathbb M_{2}[\tau_i, \xi_j, i\geq 0, j\geq 1]/(\tau_i^2=\tau\xi_{i+1} + \rho \tau_0\xi_{i+1} + \rho\tau_{i+1}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-020: The motivic dual Steenrod algebra $A_{**}$ is a Hopf algebroid.
elements.sent-2411-021: From now on, we switch to conjugate generators $\chi\tau_i$ and $\chi\xi_i$ for $A_{**}$ without changing notations, then we can rewrite (\cref{eq:dualA ) with \begin{equation}A_{**}=\mathbb M_{2}[\tau_i, \xi_j, i\geq 0, j\geq 1]/(\tau_i^2=\tau\xi_{i+1}+\rho\tau_{i+1}).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Here each relation has one less terms, which is very important in our calculation.
elements.sent-2411-023: One can express the usual motivic Milnor basis using the one used in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-024: However, as discussed in \cite{BGL , there are no obvious conjugation maps on the motivic Steenrod algebra in general.. \end{impnot.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Let $\Seq$ denote the set of all sequences of the form $(r_0, r_1,\dots)$ such that $r_i$ is non-zero for only finitely many $i$.
elements.sent-2411-026: Let $\Seqe\subset \Seq$ denote the subset of all sequences whose entries are either $0$ or $1$.
elements.sent-2411-027: Let $(n)_i$ denote the sequence whose $i$th entry is $n$ and all other entires are $0$.
elements.sent-2411-028: It is clear that \begin{equation}\{\tau_0^{e_0}\tau_1^{e_1}\cdots\cdot \xi_0^{r_0}\xi_1^{r_1}\cdots~|~ (e_i)\in \Seqe, (r_j)\in \Seq\}\end{equation} is an $\bM_2$-basis of $A_{**}$.
elements.sent-2411-029: For $E=(e_0,e_1,\dots)\in \Seqe$ and $R=(r_0,r_1,\dots)\in \Seq$, let $Q(E)$ and $P(R)$ denote the dual of $\tau(E)=\tau_0^{e_0}\tau_1^{e_1}\cdots$ and $\xi(R)= \xi_0^{r_0}\xi_1^{r_1}\cdots$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-032: The proofs are similar to those in \cite[Section 13]{Voev .}
elements.sent-2411-034: This forms an additive basis for the motivic Steenrod algebra, analogous to the classical Milnor basis \cite{Mil .}
elements.sent-2411-035: We refer to this basis as the motivic Milnor basis.
elements.sent-2411-036: Since we have switched to the conjugates without changing notations, the unit map and the coproduct map in $A_{**}$ have the following formulas.
elements.sent-2411-039: Our goal is to determine the product formula for \begin{equation}Q(E_1)P(R_1)\cdot \tau^n Q(E_2)P(R_2)\end{equation} where $E_1,E_2\in \Seqe$ and $R_1,R_2\in \Seq$.
elements.sent-2411-040: It can be deduced by analyzing the co-multiplicative structure in the motivic dual Steenrod algebra with elements $\tau(E)\xi(R)$.
elements.sent-2411-041: One of the difficulties for finding the product formula comes from the relations $\tau_i^2=\tau\xi_{i+1 + \rho\tau_{i+1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-042: For some $S\in \Seq$, we want to simplify $\tau(S)=\tau_0^{s_0}\tau_1^{s_1}\cdots$ into the form $\Sigma c_i\tau(E_i)\xi(R_i)$ where each $c_i\in \mathbb M_2$, $E_i\in \Seqe$ and $R_i\in \Seq$.
elements.sent-2411-044: Every node is labeled by two sequences $S|R$, representing $\tau(S)\xi(R).$.
elements.sent-2411-045: The sum of the monomials represented by child nodes equals the monomial represented by the parent node.
elements.sent-2411-046: The branches from a parent node to its child nodes correspond to rewriting a $\tau_i^2$ factor into $\tau\xi_{i+1 +\rho\tau_{i+1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-048: The root node is labeled by $S|0$.
elements.sent-2411-049: A node with label $S'|R$ such that $S'\in \Seq -\Seqe$ has a left child node labeled \begin{equation}S'-(2)_n+(1)_{n+1}|R,\end{equation} and a right child node labeled \begin{equation}S'-(2)_n| R+ (1)_{n+1},\end{equation} where $n$ is the smallest index with $s_n\geq 2$.
elements.sent-2411-050: If a node labeled $E|R$ satisfies $E\in \Seqe$, then it is a leaf node.
elements.sent-2411-051: Once we have created the tree, the simplified expression is \begin{equation}\tau(S) = \sum_{\substack{leaf node label  E|R}}c\cdot p(\tau,\rho)\tau(E)\xi(R),\end{equation} where $c$ is the number of occurrences of the leaf node label $E|R$, and $p(\tau,\rho)$ is a polynomial with variables $\tau$ and $\rho$ whose powers can be determined by counting degrees.
elements.sent-2411-052: For example, the simplification of $\tau((21)|(0))$ and $\tau((4)|(0))$ is represented by the following trees. \begin{equation}\begin{forest} for tree={ draw, minimum size=1em, s sep=1em, l sep=0.7em, } [$(21)|(0)$ [$(02)|(0)$ [$(001)|(0)$] [$(0)|(001)$] ] [$(01)|(01)$ ] ] \end{forest} \begin{forest} for tree={ draw, minimum size=1em, s sep=1em, l sep=0.7em, } [$(4)|(0)$ [$(21)|(0)$ [$(02)|(0)$ [$(001)|(0)$] [$(0)|(001)$] ] [$(01)|(01)$] ] [$(2)|(01)$ [$(01)|(01)$] [$(0)|(02)$] ] ] \end{forest}\end{equation} \end{strategy \begin{notation Let $R$ and $S$ be two sequences.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: \begin{equation}c(S,R)=  \prod_{n\geq 1} \binom{\lfloor\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 2^{i-n}(s_i - r_i)\rfloor}{r_n}, r_0=0 0, r_0>0 \end{equation} Here we take $\binom{m {n =0$ if $m<n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: This is because of the rewriting rule and comparing degree: every two in $e_i$ contributes to one in $s_{i+1}$ or one in $e_{i+1}$.
elements.sent-2411-056: Suppose $n$ is the largest index with a nonzero entry in $R$.
elements.sent-2411-057: Then $E|R$ is a descendant of \begin{equation}E_{n-1}|R_{n-1}= (e_0,\dots, e_{n-2},\sum_{i=0}^{n-2} 2^{i-n+1}(s_i - e_i - r_i)+s_{n-1}-r_{n-1},\dots)|(r_0,r_1,\dots, r_{n-1}, 0,\dots);\end{equation} note that by the assumption that we rewrite from the left-most re-writable entry, we can assume $e_i\in\{0,1\}$ for $i\leq n-2.$ Therefore, we have \begin{equation}\sum_{i=0}^{n-2} 2^{i-n+1}(s_i - e_i - r_i)+s_{n-1}-r_{n-1} = \lfloor\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 2^{i-n+1}(s_i - r_i)\rfloor.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-060: The final case is \begin{equation}E_{-1}|R_{-1} = S|(0),\end{equation} and we require $r_0 =0.$ We conclude that the number of occurrences is \begin{equation}c(S,R)= \prod_{n\geq 1} \binom{\lfloor\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 2^{i-n}(s_i - r_i)\rfloor}{r_n}, r_0=0 0, r_0>0 \end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-061: Note that $c(S,R)\neq 0$ implies that $r_0=0$ and for all $n\ge 0$, \begin{equation}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 2^is_i\ge \sum_{i=1}^n 2^ir_i.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-070: It's not hard to check the coefficient is $b(Y).$ 1)~~ We verify the equation by the following computation.. \begin{equation}\psi(\tau(E)) = \prod_k (1\otimes \tau_k+\sum_{i=0}^k \tau_i\otimes \xi_{k-i}^{2^i})^{e_k} = \sum_{E'+E'' = E} \prod_k \binom{e_k}{e_k'} (1\otimes \tau_k)^{e_k'} \cdot (\sum_{i=0}^k \tau_i\otimes \xi_{k-i}^{2^i})^{e^{\prime\prime}_k} = \sum_{\substack{E'+E'' = E T(Y)=E''}} \binom{E}{E'}b(Y) \cdot \tau(S(Y))\otimes \tau(E') \xi(R(Y)) = \sum_{\substack{ T(Y) \leq E}} \binom{E}{E-T(Y)}b(Y) \cdot \tau(S(Y))\otimes \tau(E-T(Y)) \xi(R(Y)).\end{equation}.

elements.section-2411-005: Product formula for the motivic Milnor basis

elements.sent-2411-079: Last, we deal with the case when the second factor has a coefficient $\tau^n$.
elements.sent-2411-081: Note that for $m\in A_{**}$ and $a\in A$, we have \begin{equation}\langle m, a\tau^n\rangle=\langle m, a\langle \tau^n, 1\rangle\rangle=\langle m\otimes \tau^n, a\otimes 1\rangle=\langle(\tau+\rho\tau_0)^nm, a\rangle.\end{equation}.
